Assets

Site assets — CSS, JavaScript, fonts, and static images — live at your site root and are referenced with absolute paths in your templates.

Where assets live

Assets live alongside your HTML templates at the site root. Organize them into directories that make sense for your project — css/, js/, images/, fonts/, or whatever structure you prefer:

my-site/
  index.html
  about.html
  partials/
    head.html
    nav.html
    footer.html
  css/
    style.css
    components.css
  js/
    main.js
  fonts/
    inter-regular.woff2
    inter-bold.woff2
  images/
    logo.svg
    og-default.jpg
    icons/
      arrow.svg
      close.svg
  outpost/

There is no asset pipeline. Files are served exactly as-is — no bundling, no minification, no content hashing. The web server delivers them directly as static files, bypassing PHP entirely. This keeps the setup simple and deployment fast.

Referencing assets in templates

Reference assets using absolute paths from the site root:

<link rel="stylesheet" href="/css/style.css">
<script src="/js/main.js"></script>
<img src="/images/logo.svg" alt="Logo">

Always use absolute paths (starting with /). Relative paths will break on any URL that is not the root — for example, a relative path in head.html works on / but fails on /about or /post/hello-world. Absolute paths work everywhere.

No asset pipeline — intentional simplicity

Outpost deliberately avoids a build step for site assets. There is no webpack, no Vite, no PostCSS, no Sass compilation. What you put in your asset directories is what gets served.

This is a deliberate design choice. A designer working on a client site should be able to open style.css, change a color, upload the file, and see the result immediately — without running a build command.

If you prefer to use a build tool for development (Vite, esbuild, Sass), you can absolutely do so — just configure its output to write to your asset directories. The final deployed files are what matter.

Cache busting

Because static files are served directly, browsers cache them aggressively. When you update style.css, visitors who have the old version cached will not see your changes until their cache expires.

The simplest solution is to add a version query string to your asset URLs:

<link rel="stylesheet" href="/css/style.css?v=2">

Increment the version number each time you deploy changes to that file. You can also use a date or build number:

<link rel="stylesheet" href="/css/style.css?v=20260330">

This manual approach is low-tech but reliable. It requires remembering to bump the version in your template — typically in partials/head.html for CSS and partials/footer.html for JS.

Tip — If you are using a build tool, configure it to include a content hash in the output filename (e.g., style.a3f9b1.css) and update the reference in your partial each build. This is the most reliable form of cache busting because the URL changes only when the content changes.

Recommended directory structure

There are no required directory names, but this structure serves most projects well:

Directory / FilePurpose
css/style.cssMain stylesheet — all site styles
js/main.jsMain JavaScript file
fonts/Self-hosted web font files (.woff2, .woff)
images/Static site images — logo, placeholder images, decorative backgrounds
images/icons/SVG icon files referenced in templates

You are not required to follow this structure. Organize your assets however makes sense for your project.

Fonts

There are two approaches to web fonts:

Google Fonts (or any CDN)

Link to the font in partials/head.html. This is the fastest approach for development — no files to manage.

<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com">
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin>
<link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Inter:wght@400;500;600&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">

Self-hosted fonts

Download the font files (in .woff2 format) and place them in fonts/. Then declare them in your stylesheet:

/* In css/style.css */
@font-face {
  font-family: 'Inter';
  src: url('/fonts/inter-regular.woff2') format('woff2');
  font-weight: 400;
  font-style: normal;
  font-display: swap;
}

@font-face {
  font-family: 'Inter';
  src: url('/fonts/inter-bold.woff2') format('woff2');
  font-weight: 700;
  font-style: normal;
  font-display: swap;
}

Use font-display: swap to prevent invisible text while the font loads.

User-uploaded images vs. site images

There is an important distinction between two kinds of images:

TypeLocationHow to use
Site static images images/ (at site root) <img src="/images/logo.svg">
User-uploaded images outpost/content/uploads/ data-outpost="hero_image" data-type="image"

Site images are part of the site's visual design — the logo, decorative backgrounds, placeholder images. They are committed alongside your templates and do not change when a site editor uploads new content.

User-uploaded images are managed through the Media Library and entered into content fields via the admin editor. They live in outpost/content/uploads/.

Note — Never store user-uploaded content in your site's asset directories. The uploads directory (outpost/content/uploads/) is managed by the CMS and persists across updates.

SVG icons in templates

Small SVG icons are often inlined directly in HTML for performance and to allow CSS styling. For larger SVG files like logos, the <img> tag is cleaner. Either approach works in Outpost templates:

<!-- External SVG via img tag -->
<img src="/images/icons/arrow.svg" alt="">

<!-- Inline SVG (paste SVG code directly) -->
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 24 24">
  <path d="M5 12h14M12 5l7 7-7 7"/>
</svg>
